items.question: What is matrix multiplication in linear algebra?
items.answer: Matrix multiplication is the operation that takes an m x n matrix A and an n x p matrix B and produces an m x p matrix C, where each entry Ci,j is computed as the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.

items.question: Why is matrix multiplication defined with the row-by-column rule instead of element-wise multiplication?
items.answer: The row-by-column rule makes matrix multiplication correspond exactly to composition of linear transformations, so multiplying matrices matches combining linear maps and preserves key algebraic properties like associativity and distributivity that underlie most of linear algebra.

items.question: Who uses matrix multiplication heavily in practice?
items.answer: Matrix multiplication is used heavily by data scientists, machine learning engineers, numerical analysts, physicists, computer graphics programmers and anyone working with large linear systems, where it powers operations on batches of vectors and compact representations of complex transformations.

items.question: Where do matrix multiplications typically appear in modern ML systems?
items.answer: Matrix multiplications appear throughout ML systems in linear and logistic regression, dense neural network layers, attention mechanisms implemented as batched matrix products, and in the linear algebra routines used in optimisation algorithms and factorisations.

items.question: When can two matrices A and B be multiplied as AB?
items.answer: Two matrices A and B can be multiplied as AB exactly when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B; this shared size is the inner dimension that determines the length of the dot products defining each entry of the product.

items.question: How do you compute a specific entry Ci,j of the product C = AB?
items.answer: To compute Ci,j you take row i of A and column j of B, multiply corresponding entries and sum them: Ci,j = sum over k of Ai,k * Bk,j, where k runs over the inner dimension.

items.question: What breaks if you try to multiply A 2 x 3 and B 4 x 2 as AB without changing either matrix?
items.answer: The multiplication AB is not defined because the inner dimensions do not match: A has 3 columns but B has 4 rows, so you cannot form the required dot products between rows of A and columns of B.

items.question: What breaks if you use element-wise multiplication instead of matrix multiplication in a linear model, computing X * W instead of XW?
items.answer: Element-wise multiplication requires X and W to have the same shape and does not sum across features, so each output entry depends only on a single feature. This destroys the intended linear combination of features and changes the model into a much less expressive one.

items.question: What breaks if you swap the order of two transformation matrices R (rotation) and S (scaling) and use RS instead of SR in a graphics pipeline?
items.answer: Swapping the order changes the transformation applied to points because rotation and scaling do not commute; objects may appear stretched in the wrong direction or rotated about an unintended axis, breaking the visual correctness of the rendered scene.
